methods.description: This method employs the Generalized Pseudospectral (GPS) scheme to solve the radial Schrödinger equation for a system with a Morse potential. It utilizes a nonuniform optimal spatial discretization based on Legendre pseudospectral methods and an algebraic nonlinear mapping to transform the problem into a symmetric matrix eigenvalue problem, which is then solved to obtain accurate eigenvalues and eigenfunctions.

steps.description: The desired radial Schrödinger equation can be written in the following operator form, where the Hamiltonian operator includes usual kinetic and potential energy operators, Here ℓ denotes the usual angular momentum quantum number identifying rotational states. Last term in Eq. (2) represents the Morse potential. Potential strength D e > 0, x = (r − r e )/r e , and α, r e are two positive parameters.
steps.searchable_summary: Define the radial Schrödinger equation with kinetic, angular momentum, and Morse potential terms.
outputs: Mathematical formulation of the radial Schrödinger equation

steps.description: A primary feature of this scheme is that a function f (x) in the interval x ∈ [−1, 1] can be approximated by an N-th order polynomial f N (x) as given below, This approximation is exact at the collocation points x j , i.e., f N (x j ) = f (x j ). In the Legendre pseudospectral method used in this work we also have, x 0 = −1, x N = 1, while x j (j = 1, . . . , N − 1) are obtained from roots of first derivative of P N (x) with respect to x, i.e., (3) are given by following expression, thereby satisfying a unique property g j (
steps.searchable_summary: Define polynomial approximation using Legendre pseudospectral method and determine collocation points.

steps.description: Now invoking an algebraic nonlinear mapping of the form, r = r(x) = L 1+x 1−x+α , with L and α = 2L/r max as mapping parameters, and introducing a symmetrization procedure leads to a transformed Hamiltonian as below, The advantage is that this leads to a symmetric matrix eigenvalue problem which can be readily solved to obtain accurate eigenvalues and eigenfunctions. For the particular transformation used above, v m (x) = 0. So, finally one obtains the following set of coupled equations, where with
steps.searchable_summary: Apply nonlinear coordinate mapping and symmetrization to transform the Hamiltonian into a symmetric matrix form.
outputs: Coupled equations for the matrix eigenvalue problem

steps.description: The advantage is that this leads to a symmetric matrix eigenvalue problem which can be readily solved to obtain accurate eigenvalues and eigenfunctions.
steps.searchable_summary: Solve the resulting symmetric matrix eigenvalue problem.
inputs: Symmetric matrix representation of the transformed Hamiltonian

steps.description: A sufficiently large number of test calculations were done to check the performance of this method with respect to the mapping parameters. Results are reported here only up to the precision that were found to maintain stability with such variations. In this way, a consistent set of numerical parameters of α = 25, N = 300 were chosen, which seemed to be appropriate and satisfactory for our purposes. In general, r max = 200 a.u. was sufficient for most calculations, but for higher excited states, it needed to be increased to capture the long-range tails in wave function.
steps.searchable_summary: Select appropriate numerical parameters for the calculation based on testing.
inputs: Results from test calculations with varying parameters
outputs: Chosen numerical parameters (e.g., N, mapping parameters α, L, r_max)

methods.description: This procedure validates the accuracy and reliability of the eigenvalues calculated using the GPS method by comparing them against known exact analytical solutions (for s-wave states) and published results from other established numerical and analytical methods for various molecules and quantum states.

steps.description: A sufficiently large number of test calculations were done to check the performance of this method with respect to the mapping parameters. Results are reported here only up to the precision that were found to maintain stability with such variations.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform test calculations with varying parameters to assess method performance and stability.
inputs: Range of numerical parameters (e.g., N, mapping parameters)
outputs: Calculated eigenvalues/eigenfunctions for different parameter sets

steps.description: In this way, a consistent set of numerical parameters of α = 25, N = 300 were chosen, which seemed to be appropriate and satisfactory for our purposes. In general, r max = 200 a.u. was sufficient for most calculations, but for higher excited states, it needed to be increased to capture the long-range tails in wave function.
steps.searchable_summary: Select a consistent set of numerical parameters based on test calculations.
outputs: Chosen numerical parameters (e.g., N, mapping parameters α, L, r_max)

steps.description: Note that, s-wave eigenstates offer exact analytical results and thus can be used as a valuable guide to assess the quality of our computed energies. It is observed that, GPS results for all ℓ = 0 states match perfectly with the exact results. As these are available in [23], they are not quoted here again to avoid repetition.
steps.searchable_summary: Compare calculated s-wave (ℓ=0) eigenvalues with exact analytical results.
inputs: Calculated s-wave eigenvalues from GPS method
outputs: Assessment of accuracy for s-wave states

steps.description: It is quite gratifying that, for all the states, our present GPS results exactly reproduce these accurate eigenvalues [from Ref 23]. As a further test of the performance of GPS scheme, Table 3 compares energies of some high-lying states (ℓ = 10, 20, 25; n = 0 − 5) of H 2 , LiH, HCl and CO with literature results. ... However, as in Table 1, in this case also, it appears that the most accurate energies are offered by J-matrix basis that facilitates a tridiagonal representation of the reference Hamiltonian [23]. For H 2 and CO, it is seen that agreement of present eigenvalues with that of [23] is excellent... Unexpectedly however, for LiH and HCl, these two energies show significant deviation from each other. ... From a careful observation of these two tables in [23], it is clear that the three common s-wave energies (namely E 0,0 , E 5,0 , E 7,0 ) differ from each other with respect to changes in the parameter λ. Since our s-wave results practically coincide with the exact results (quoted in Table 2 of [23]) and also with their own calculations in Table 2, the slight differences in our results from those in [23] for the rotational case of HCl may be attributed to the different parameter sets employed by them. We anticipated a similar phenomenon for LiH. ... In any case, keeping in mind the excellent accuracy and reliability GPS method has offered for all the above states in various molecules and also in many other previous situations [29][30][31][32][33][34][35][36][37], we are led to conclude that the results for LiH are also as accurate as for the other three molecules.
steps.searchable_summary: Compare calculated rotational (ℓ>0) eigenvalues with published results from other methods, analyze deviations.
inputs: Calculated rotational state eigenvalues from GPS method
outputs: Comparison results (e.g., tables of values, deviations)

steps.description: Once the accuracy and reliability of our method is established, we now investigate the variation of energies in different states. For that, in Fig While the ℓ axis in (d)-(f) has been kept fixed at 25, the n axis in (a)-(c), on the other hand, has been varied to capture the necessary qualitative structures in these plots.
steps.searchable_summary: Visualize the variation of calculated energies with quantum numbers n and ℓ.
inputs: Calculated eigenvalues (energies) for various n and ℓ states
outputs: Plots showing energy vs n and energy vs ℓ
